learning_objectives: Describe the scope and methods of health informatics research
Differentiate between clinical, technical, and sociotechnical research approaches
Understand the research-to-practice pipeline in health informatics
readings: Research Methods in Health Informatics Ch. 1
Friedman CP. A Science of Health Informatics. JAMIA 2009

lecture_notes.introduction: Health informatics is an applied discipline where research directly influences practice. Understanding research methods enables you to evaluate evidence, design studies, and contribute to the knowledge base.
key_concepts: Health informatics as a discipline: the science of how data, information, and knowledge are used in healthcare
Research paradigms: experimental, observational, design science, qualitative, mixed methods
Sociotechnical systems theory: technology in the context of people, processes, and organizations
Translational informatics research: from concept to implementation to impact
Key informatics research journals: JAMIA, JMIR, IJMI, Applied Clinical Informatics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Critically appraise a published health informatics study: identify the research question, methodology, findings, and limitations

review.value: Framework recognizing that health IT success depends on the interaction between technology, people, workflow, and organizational context - not technology alone
Research paradigm focused on creating and evaluating IT artifacts (systems, methods, frameworks) to solve identified problems

learning_objectives: Select appropriate study designs for health informatics research questions
Understand the hierarchy of evidence as applied to informatics
Design studies that account for the complexity of healthcare settings
readings: Research Methods in Health Informatics Ch. 3-4
Curran GM. Effectiveness-Implementation Hybrid Designs. Medical Care 2012

lecture_notes.introduction: Choosing the right study design is critical for producing valid, actionable research. Health informatics research presents unique design challenges including rapid technology change and complex care environments.
key_concepts: Randomized controlled trials for health IT interventions
Quasi-experimental designs: pre-post, interrupted time series, stepped-wedge
Observational studies: cohort, case-control, cross-sectional in informatics contexts
Usability studies: think-aloud protocols, heuristic evaluation, cognitive walkthrough
Implementation science hybrid designs: studying effectiveness and implementation simultaneously
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial to evaluate a new CDS intervention across 6 hospital units

review.value: Cluster randomized design where all clusters eventually receive the intervention, randomized by the time they cross over - practical for health IT rollouts
Quasi-experimental design analyzing trends before and after an intervention, controlling for pre-existing trends - common for evaluating health IT implementations

learning_objectives: Apply appropriate statistical methods to health informatics data
Conduct sample size calculations for informatics studies
Interpret and report statistical results correctly
readings: Research Methods in Health Informatics Ch. 6
Hersh W. A Stimulus to Define Informatics and Health IT. BMC Medical Informatics 2009

lecture_notes.introduction: Quantitative methods provide the statistical rigor needed to determine whether health IT interventions actually improve outcomes. Choosing the right analytical approach is critical.
key_concepts: Regression analysis for health IT outcome studies (linear, logistic, multilevel)
Time series analysis for pre-post implementation studies (ARIMA, segmented regression)
Propensity score methods for observational health IT studies
Sample size and power calculations for cluster-level interventions
Multiple comparisons and the Bonferroni problem in EHR data analysis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct an interrupted time series analysis of medication error rates before and after CPOE implementation using segmented regression in Python/R

review.value: Statistical method for interrupted time series: models the trend before intervention, the immediate level change at intervention, and the trend change after intervention
Statistical approach accounting for hierarchical data structure (patients within clinics within hospitals) common in multi-site health IT evaluations

learning_objectives: Design qualitative studies for understanding health IT adoption and use
Apply thematic analysis and grounded theory to interview data
Integrate qualitative and quantitative methods in mixed methods designs
readings: Research Methods in Health Informatics Ch. 7
Creswell JW. Mixed Methods Research in Health Informatics

lecture_notes.introduction: Numbers alone cannot explain why clinicians resist a new EHR or how workflows actually change after implementation. Qualitative methods provide the 'why' behind the 'what' in informatics research.
key_concepts: Qualitative approaches: interviews, focus groups, observation, document analysis
Data analysis methods: thematic analysis, grounded theory, content analysis
Qualitative rigor: trustworthiness, credibility, transferability, confirmability
Mixed methods designs: convergent, explanatory sequential, exploratory sequential
Qualitative studies of EHR implementation, workarounds, and unintended consequences
lecture_notes.demonstration: Code a set of clinician interview transcripts about EHR usability using thematic analysis, developing initial codes and themes
setup_requirements: Qualitative data analysis software (NVivo or ATLAS.ti - free alternatives available)

review.value: Systematic method for identifying, analyzing, and reporting patterns (themes) across qualitative data - widely used in informatics to understand user experiences
First collect and analyze quantitative data (e.g., EHR usage logs), then collect qualitative data (interviews) to explain the quantitative findings

learning_objectives: Navigate IRB requirements for health informatics studies
Understand special considerations for research using EHR data
Evaluate data use agreements and de-identification requirements
readings: Research Methods in Health Informatics Ch. 9
OHRP Guidance on EHR Data for Research

lecture_notes.introduction: Research using health data raises unique ethical challenges around consent, privacy, and the secondary use of clinical information. IRB navigation and ethical data stewardship are essential skills.
key_concepts: Common Rule and HIPAA research provisions
IRB review categories: exempt, expedited, full board for informatics studies
Waiver of informed consent for retrospective EHR data studies
De-identification for research: Safe Harbor vs. Expert Determination
Data Use Agreements and Limited Data Sets
Ethical considerations in AI/ML model development using patient data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Complete an IRB application for a retrospective study analyzing CDS alert override patterns using EHR log data

review.value: IRB can waive informed consent for retrospective research when: minimal risk, cannot practicably be done with consent, does not adversely affect rights
PHI with direct identifiers removed but retaining dates and geographic information - requires Data Use Agreement for research use

class_schedule.topic: Writing and Publishing Health Informatics Research
learning_objectives: Structure a health informatics research manuscript
Understand peer review and the publication process
Apply reporting guidelines (CONSORT, STROBE, STARE-HI) to informatics studies
readings: Research Methods in Health Informatics Ch. 11
Talmon J. STARE-HI Reporting Guidelines for Health Informatics

lecture_notes.introduction: Research that is not published does not advance the field. Learning to write clearly, follow reporting guidelines, and navigate peer review is essential for contributing to health informatics knowledge.
key_concepts: Manuscript structure: IMRAD (Introduction, Methods, Results, Discussion) for informatics
STARE-HI: reporting guidelines specific to health informatics evaluations
CONSORT for RCTs, STROBE for observational studies, PRISMA for systematic reviews
Journal selection: matching your research to the right audience and journal
Peer review process: responding to reviewer comments effectively
lecture_notes.demonstration: Outline a research manuscript based on a completed study, applying STARE-HI reporting guidelines
setup_requirements: Sample published informatics paper for analysis

review.value: Statement on Reporting of Evaluation Studies in Health Informatics: checklist ensuring informatics evaluations report system description, methods, results, and lessons learned completely
Introduction (why), Methods (how), Results (what was found), And Discussion (what it means) - standard structure for scientific manuscripts

questions.question: A hospital wants to evaluate whether a new sepsis CDS alert reduces 30-day mortality. Propose a study design, justify your choice, and identify at least two threats to internal validity.
questions.answer: Recommended design: Stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial. Justification: A traditional RCT is impractical because the CDS alert is deployed at the unit level (not individual patient level), and ethically it is difficult to withhold a potentially life-saving intervention long-term. The stepped-wedge design allows all units to eventually receive the intervention while providing randomized control data from units that have not yet crossed over. Threats to internal validity: (1) Temporal confounding: mortality rates may change over time due to seasonal factors (e.g., flu season) independent of the CDS intervention. Mitigation: include time as a covariate. (2) Contamination: clinicians working on both intervention and control units may change their behavior on control units. Mitigation: cluster at the hospital or floor level to minimize cross-contamination.

rubric.key: Two valid threats to internal validity

questions.question: Explain why interrupted time series (ITS) analysis is particularly well-suited for evaluating health IT implementations. What are its advantages and limitations compared to a simple pre-post comparison?
questions.answer: ITS is well-suited because health IT implementations are typically deployed at a specific time point and affect all patients in the system, making individual randomization impossible. Advantages over simple pre-post: (1) Controls for pre-existing trends - if medication errors were already declining before CPOE implementation, a pre-post comparison would falsely attribute the entire decline to CPOE. ITS separates the pre-existing trend from the intervention effect. (2) Detects both immediate level changes and gradual slope changes - the intervention may have an immediate impact and/or a gradual learning effect. (3) Provides multiple data points before and after, increasing statistical power and credibility. Limitations: (1) Cannot account for concurrent events (a new pharmacy policy implemented at the same time as CPOE). (2) Requires sufficient pre- and post-intervention data points (typically 8+ each). (3) Assumes no other major changes occurred simultaneously.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of ITS suitability for health IT
At least 2 advantages over pre-post comparison

questions.question: Describe a mixed methods research design to study EHR usability and its impact on clinician burnout. What quantitative and qualitative data would you collect, and how would you integrate them?
questions.answer: Design: Explanatory sequential mixed methods. Phase 1 (Quantitative): Collect EHR usage log data (clicks per encounter, time in notes, after-hours EHR use), clinician burnout survey (Maslach Burnout Inventory), and EHR usability survey (System Usability Scale). Analyze correlations between EHR usage patterns and burnout scores, identifying high-burden user subgroups. Phase 2 (Qualitative): Conduct semi-structured interviews with 15-20 clinicians purposefully sampled from high-burnout and low-burnout groups identified in Phase 1. Ask about specific pain points, workarounds, and what features help or hinder their work. Analyze using thematic analysis. Integration: Use qualitative findings to explain quantitative results. For example, if after-hours EHR use correlates with burnout, interviews may reveal specific documentation requirements driving 'pajama time.' The integrated findings inform targeted EHR optimization recommendations.

rubric.key: Appropriate mixed methods design with both phases described

questions.question: A researcher wants to study patterns in CDS alert overrides using 5 years of EHR audit log data from 3 hospitals. What IRB considerations apply? Can consent be waived?
questions.answer: IRB considerations: (1) This is a retrospective study using existing data (EHR audit logs), likely qualifying for expedited review or exempt status depending on identifiability. (2) The audit logs contain provider identifiers (who overrode alerts) and patient identifiers (whose records were accessed), making this PHI under HIPAA. (3) Consent waiver is likely appropriate under 45 CFR 46.116(f) because: (a) the research involves no more than minimal risk, (b) the waiver will not adversely affect rights and welfare of subjects, (c) the research could not practicably be carried out without the waiver (contacting all clinicians and patients over 5 years is infeasible), (d) subjects will be provided relevant information after participation (if appropriate). (4) Under HIPAA, the researcher needs either: a waiver of authorization from the IRB/Privacy Board, a limited data set with DUA, or a fully de-identified dataset. (5) Multi-site considerations: each hospital's IRB must approve, or a single IRB arrangement can be used.

rubric.key: Accurate consent waiver analysis with regulatory basis

major_assignments.title: Health Informatics Research Proposal
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete research proposal for a health informatics study, including literature review, study design, methodology, analysis plan, ethical considerations, and timeline.
objectives: Formulate a researchable health informatics question
Design an appropriate study with rigorous methodology
Address ethical and regulatory requirements for health data research
Write a proposal suitable for IRB submission or grant application
deliverables: Research proposal (15-20 pages) following NIH R21 format
Literature review with at least 20 peer-reviewed sources
Detailed methodology section with sample size justification

criteria: Clear, focused, and answerable research question
Rigorous sampling and data collection methods
Sound analysis plan addressing validity threats
Complete ethical consideration and professional writing

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of research methods concepts and critical appraisal skills

journal_club.description: Critical appraisal presentations of published informatics research

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of study design and quantitative methods

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including qualitative methods, ethics, and research writing

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. Journal club presentations are mandatory. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 10% per day. Research proposal milestones must be met for iterative feedback.
course_policies.collaboration: Literature search strategies may be discussed. All written work must be individual.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All research proposals must be original. Plagiarism in academic writing will result in course failure. Proper APA citation required throughout.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Statistical software (Python or R), qualitative analysis software, PubMed/library database access, reference management software (Zotero or Mendeley).
